title: Data-Efficient Indentation Size Effect Correction in Steels Using Machine Learning and Physics-Guided Augmentation
abstract: Shallow nanoindentation enables mechanical characterization of thin films, individual phases and other volume-constrained materials, but measured hardness is often inflated by the indentation size effect (ISE), contact-area errors and tip-geometry artifacts. Classical ISE corrections such as the Nix-Gao require a deep linear regime and are unreliable when only shallow measurements are used. This study investigates how a small experimental dataset can be used to predict a reference hardness with physics-guided feature engineering and augmentation. Approximately 700 experimental indentations were collected from three steel reference specimens covering a hardness range of 2-6.5 GPa and augmented using physically motivated variations representing instrumental noise, session-level drift, and local multiphase boundary blending. The input space combined Oliver-Pharr values with mechanics descriptors, including indentation work partitioning, ($H\text{/}E_{r}$), and the area-invariant compliance proxy ($P_{\max}\text{/}S^{2}$). Ridge Regression (RR), Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks (NN) were evaluated using a quarantined fourth steel specimen tested at staggered loads. The hardness mapping was nonlinear: RR failed, whereas nonlinear models achieved ($R^2 > 0.98$) internally. A constrained (64-8-64) NN gave the best results, reaching RMSE = 0.470 GPa, MAPE = 5.4% on the quarantined steel. Unlike Nix-Gao analysis, the NN produced stable estimates in the shallow regime. SHAP and latent-space analysis showed reliance on area-invariant and energy-based descriptors. The results demonstrate the feasibility of a this workflow for ISE correction in steels using small datasets and suggest a pathway toward data-efficient characterization of any volume constrained materials.
summary: This paper presents a machine learning approach to correct hardness measurements in steels that are otherwise distorted by small-scale effects. By using physics-informed features and data augmentation, the authors developed a neural network that can accurately predict bulk material properties from very shallow indentation tests.
main_problem: Correcting the Indentation Size Effect (ISE) in nanoindentation, where measured hardness is artificially inflated at shallow depths due to strain-gradient plasticity and geometric artifacts like pile-up.
main_result: A constrained bottleneck neural network (64-8-64) successfully predicts bulk hardness from shallow-depth data with high accuracy (MAPE = 5.4%), outperforming classical Nix-Gao models and tree-based regressors.
method: A physics-guided machine learning framework using data augmentation (Gaussian noise, drift, and MixUp) and feature engineering with area-invariant mechanical descriptors.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of multiphase steels with varying microstructures (ferritic/pearlitic to martensitic). The ML models evaluated include Ridge Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and constrained Neural Networks.
key_observables: Hardness (H), reduced elastic modulus (Er), contact depth (hc), maximum load (Pmax), contact stiffness (S), and plastic work fraction (Wp/Wtot).
important_parameters: Shallow contact regime (hc < 215 nm), hardness range 2-6.5 GPa, and the use of area-invariant proxies like Pmax/S^2.
main_assumptions: The training steels share a constrained chemical composition; the model is not universal and fails on amorphous materials like fused silica.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows depth-dependent hardness and SEM images of pile-up; Figure 2 compares Nix-Gao errors; Figure 3 shows model parity plots; Figure 5 compares RF and NN performance on unseen specimens; Figure 6 shows model failure on fused silica.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ISE problem, describes the experimental nanoindentation setup and steel specimens, details the physics-guided data augmentation and feature engineering, evaluates various ML architectures, performs interpretability analysis via SHAP and PCA, and concludes with limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Depth-dependent apparent hardness and representative shallow indentation curve. (a) Apparent Oliver–Pharr hardness as a function of contact depth for the three training steels. (b) Representative low-load (P)–(h) curve showing unloading fit, stiffness tangent, and contact-depth estimate. (c) SEM images of indents of E841 specimen; image blur at 10 and 5 mN reflects instrument resolution limits.

Figure 2: Baseline evaluation of the classical Nix-Gao model, demonstrating that forcing a linear extrapolation through the full dataset causes non-physical overestimations of hardness (MAPE = 8.0%). Predictive accuracy is only restored (MAPE = 1.2%) when highly size-affected shallow measurements are manually truncated.

Figure 3: Parity plots comparing the predicted macroscopic bulk hardness against the experimental ground truth for the a Ridge Regression, b Random Forest, c XGBoost, and d constrained Neural Network architectures.

Figure 4: Per-load prediction error (∆H) relative to the 350 mN macroscopic reference for the E212 training steel, illustrating the baseline correction stability of the evaluated architectures across the complete indentation load range.

Figure 5: Application of the ML framework to the unseen validation specimen. Depth-dependent scatter plot comparing the highly size-affected experimental hardness (blue circles) against the size-corrected ML predictions (red squares), alongside the truncated Nix-Gao and macroscopic baselines for (a) Random Forest, (b) Neural Network. (c) Per-load prediction error (∆H) relative to the 375 mN macroscopic reference, comparing the boundary stability of the four evaluated architectures.
